title: Local-Global Compatibility in the p-Adic Langlands Progra for GL(2) over Q
speaker: Matthew Emerton
abstract: I will outline the proof of various cases of the local-global compatibility statement alluded to in the title, and also explain its applications to the Fontaine--Mazur conjecture, and to a conjecture of Kisin.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from p-adic interpolation of Langlands reciprocity
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recasting classical Langlands reciprocity through Hodge-theoretic conditions, then motivates the p-adic Langlands program by observing that Galois representations form rigid p-adic families with dense motivic points and by demanding an automorphic object carrying GL(2,Q_p)-representation theory.

proof_stages.label: Completed cohomology as the automorphic substitute
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the p-adically completed first cohomology of modular curves with all p-power levels and varying away-from-p level, and records its commuting actions by G_Q, GL(2,Q_p), the away-from-p local groups, and the Hecke algebra.

proof_stages.label: Localization at a residual Galois representation
proof_stages.summary: The completed Hecke algebra is decomposed into local factors T_m, each giving a semisimple residual representation rho_bar via the Deligne Hecke-polynomial relation, and completed cohomology is localized at the factor labelled by rho_bar.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural tensor product local-global compatibility
proof_stages.summary: The desired description of localized completed cohomology is formulated as an interpolation of classical local-global compatibility, with factors given by the universal two-dimensional Galois representation, the p-adic local Langlands representation at p, and an away-from-p factor X.

proof_stages.label: Construction constraints on the away-from-p factor
proof_stages.summary: The away-from-p factor X is explained as an interpolation of classical local Langlands which must use generic reducible inductions rather than only irreducible quotients and must be understood on a cofree or dual side because the completed object is full of Hecke torsion.

proof_stages.label: Specialization and Fontaine-Mazur consequences
proof_stages.summary: Specializing the conjectural compatibility at a p-adic lift of rho_bar gives eigenspaces whose p-adic local Langlands factor has locally algebraic vectors when the lift is de Rham with distinct Hodge-Tate weights, leading via classicality of locally algebraic vectors to Fontaine-Mazur-type modularity results.

proof_stages.label: Precise theorem, theta replacement, and scope
proof_stages.summary: Under p>2, Taylor-Wiles and local residual hypotheses, the unrestricted deformation ring is identified with the localized Hecke algebra and completed cohomology admits the asserted tensor product decomposition with a Banach GL(2,Q_p)-module theta having the correct p-adic local Langlands fibers; the subsequent discussion explains coefficient choices, the role of irreducibility, and the greater complexity of Eisenstein cases.

examples.name: GL(1) character space of Z_p^*
examples.note: The character space of Z_p^* is described as a finite union of p-adic disks with algebraic characters finite-order times x^k, illustrating how algebraic points can be dense near arithmetic points while still leaving open regions without such points.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the GL(1) case by decomposing Z_p^*, identifying the character space as a finite union of disks, and discussing algebraic points and their density/open gaps.

examples.name: Ordinary forms and Hecke characters mixing p and infinity
examples.note: Hida's ordinary condition and the passage from Hecke characters to p-adic Galois characters are used as evidence that a p-adic theory should combine the roles of the prime p and the archimedean place.
examples.engagement_reason: Ordinary forms and Hecke characters are invoked as phenomena where p-adic theory mixes p-adic and archimedean data, but no concrete calculation or construction is carried out on them.

passes.reason: ordinary forms are the unit-root/slope-zero subfamily used positively to illustrate p-infinity mixing
Ordinary forms have the slope-zero/unit-root condition, a minimal p-adic-slope case, and are used as positive evidence for p-infinity mixing.
Ordinary forms are the slope-zero/unit-root part of the p-adic family and are cited positively as evidence for mixing the p-adic and Archimedean parameters.
Ordinary forms are the slope-zero/minimal-slope part of the p-adic family and are used as a positive hint of p/infinity mixing.
Ordinary forms occupy the slope-zero/unit-root extreme and are used as motivation for p-adic mixing of the p and archimedean parameters.

examples.name: p-adic completion of Q_p versus a countable direct sum of Z_p
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the p-adic completion of Q_p as a Z_p-module, which vanishes, with the completion of a countable direct sum of Z_p's, which gives a c_0-type space, to make the completed-cohomology construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies p-adic completion to the specific modules Q_p and a countable direct sum of Z_p, saying the former completes to zero and the latter to a c_0-type space.

passes.reason: Q_p's p-adic completion collapses to zero, cautioning that countable generation alone need not survive completion

The module Q_p has p^nQ_p=Q_p so its p-adic completion collapses to zero, warning that completion of a countably generated module can fail badly.
The Q_p example has p-adic completion collapsing to zero, contrasted with the noncollapsed completion of a countable free sum.

The Q_p side has p inverted so its p-adic completion collapses to zero, contrasted with the non-collapsing countable free sum as an explanatory analogy.
The Q_p side p-adically completes to the zero module, illustrating a collapsed completion.

Used to warn that p-adic completion can kill a nonzero countable module, since the p-divisible module Q_p completes to the minimal zero object.

examples.name: Steinberg family degenerating to a split local Galois representation
examples.note: A family of semistable local Galois representations whose local Langlands images are twists of Steinberg but degenerate to a one-dimensional quotient forces the replacement of irreducible Langlands quotients by generic inductions and explains why the interpolating away-from-p factor is not simply free.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces a semistable family degenerating to a split representation through local Langlands, explaining the Steinberg degeneration, replacement by an induction, and the resulting non-freeness issue.

passes.reason: the split extension is the zero-extension limit of non-split Steinberg points and obstructs naive local-Langlands interpolation
The zero-extension split limit is a degenerate boundary point that obstructs naive interpolation of irreducible local Langlands quotients.
The split limit of a non-split Steinberg-type family is a reducibility boundary where naive interpolation by irreducible local Langlands quotients breaks down.
The split extension is the zero-extension boundary of the Steinberg family and shows naive irreducible local-Langlands interpolation fails.
The split-extension limit is a collapsed boundary point of a Steinberg family and obstructs naive interpolation by irreducible local Langlands quotients.

The split limit of a non-split Steinberg family is a boundary degeneration that defeats naive interpolation by irreducible local Langlands.
The split special fibre is a zero-extension limiting case used to show naive interpolation of classical local Langlands fails.
The split specialization is a reducible limiting degeneration used to show irreducible classical local Langlands quotients do not interpolate in families.
Used as an obstruction to naive interpolation of irreducible local Langlands, at a limiting split-extension specialization of a Steinberg family.

examples.name: Eisenstein residual case as a boundary case
examples.note: In response to a question, reducible/Eisenstein residual representations are cited as cases where the clean tensor product of local factors can fail and extra Steinberg-type phenomena may appear, illustrating the scope limitations of the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The Eisenstein residual case is cited as a more complicated boundary case with extra Steinberg factors and endoscopic analogies, but the talk does not work through a specific Eisenstein example.
